title: Austrian monetary thought
summary: The intellectual heritage that the monetary case for Bitcoin draws on. Sound money, the regression theorem, the Cantillon effect, why a fixed supply matters, the long historical record of fiat dilution.

edges.label: What is sound money
edges.summary: The Austrian definition of sound money — money chosen by the market, not decreed by the state. Hard-to-produce, durable, divisible, scarce. Why this category exists and what it rules out.

edges.label: Mises's regression theorem and Bitcoin
edges.summary: Why Mises argued that any money must regress to a prior non-monetary use value, and the long-running argument over whether Bitcoin satisfies the theorem or refutes it.

edges.label: The Cantillon effect, inflation, and malinvestment
edges.summary: Cantillon's insight that new money helps whoever spends it first, the Austrian theory of the business cycle, why credit expansion produces malinvestment booms followed by busts.

edges.label: Fiat dilution and the historical record
edges.summary: The long historical record of debasement — denarii, livres, continentals, dollars. Why every fiat currency in history has eventually been inflated, and what that pattern implies.

edges.label: Why a fixed supply matters
edges.summary: The 21 million cap, halvings as a Schelling point, why the supply schedule was set in stone at genesis and what would have to be true for it to ever change.

edges.label: Bitcoin as a unit of account and Gresham's law
edges.summary: Why bitcoin is rarely used as a unit of account today, the role of Gresham's law in explaining that, and what the 'medium of exchange first or store of value first' debate is really about.
